negativeExample: T100 accepts requestedTier from the client payload and uses it to assign the membership tier
positiveExample: T100 reads xiigen-subscriptions (GLOBAL) to determine assignedTier. requestedTier is NOT in T100 input shape — it has no downstream use.
teachingPoint: Subscription tier is a server-side authority. The client never decides its own tier. Reading requestedTier from client input allows tier escalation — a user submits requestedTier=PREMIUM while having a FREE subscription. The only source of truth is the subscription record.
discriminatingConstraint: T100 input shape has no requestedTier field. assignedTier is derived from xiigen-subscriptions lookup. There is no code path where client input influences tier assignment.
keywords: server side tier assignment subscription authority trust boundary requestedTier forbidden client payload

negativeExample: T104 (ContentFeedSeeder) triggers on MemberJoinRequested — it runs in parallel with T101 which provisions access control
positiveExample: T104 triggers on MembershipActivated (T101's output). Access control is provisioned BEFORE any content is seeded. T104 never triggers on MemberJoinRequested.
teachingPoint: Access control provisioning must complete before content access begins. If content seeding triggers on the same event as access provisioning, content may be served before the user has been granted access. The seeder must trigger on the provisioner's output event.
discriminatingConstraint: T104 @EventPattern = MembershipActivated (T101's output). T104 does not trigger on MemberJoinRequested. There is no code path where content is seeded before access control is provisioned.
keywords: access control ordering topology constraint content before access provisioned MembershipActivated trigger

negativeExample: T105 retrieves all content then post-filters for tier in application code
positiveExample: T105 passes content_access_level IN accessLevels[] as a BuildSearchFilter param. Tier gate is at the query layer — over-retrieval never happens.
teachingPoint: Tier-gated queries must filter at the query layer, not in application code. Application-layer post-filtering retrieves all documents (including higher-tier content) then discards them. This leaks data through logs, wastes bandwidth, and may expose content via timing attacks.
discriminatingConstraint: T105 query includes content_access_level: accessLevels[] in BuildSearchFilter. Application code never receives higher-tier content to discard. Cursor ceiling = 50.
keywords: tier gated query layer filter historical seed content access level BuildSearchFilter over-retrieval

negativeExample: T102 emits MembershipActivated for all groups — private groups are handled downstream
positiveExample: T102 reads group.type from DB. PRIVATE group → PENDING state → emit MembershipPending (not MembershipActivated). No content is seeded, no welcome notification sent.
teachingPoint: Private group membership requires explicit approval. A join to a private group produces PENDING state — not ACTIVE. MembershipActivated must never be emitted for private groups without admin approval. All downstream branches (content, notification) depend on MembershipActivated and must not fire for PENDING members.
discriminatingConstraint: T102 reads group.type from xiigen-groups. PRIVATE group: store membership with status=PENDING, emit MembershipPending. MembershipActivated is NOT emitted. There is no code path where a private group member receives content before approval.
keywords: private group pending state approval flow MembershipPending not activated downstream prevention

negativeExample: T114 uses SETNX on the approval record — second APPROVE returns duplicate-key failure
positiveExample: T114 uses conditional update WHERE status=PENDING. If record is already ACTIVE: second APPROVE is idempotent success. SETNX would prevent the second approval from succeeding.
teachingPoint: Approval handlers must use conditional update (WHERE status=PENDING), not SETNX. SETNX prevents duplicate creation but also prevents idempotent re-approval of an already-ACTIVE record, which is a valid success. The condition is the current state, not absence of the record.
discriminatingConstraint: T114 update condition: WHERE status=PENDING. If record status=ACTIVE: return success (already approved). T117 update condition: WHERE current_tier=oldTier. Neither uses SETNX.
keywords: conditional update SETNX status transition approval idempotent WHERE pending active

negativeExample: Wait for all branches (A=activation, B=feed, C=notification) before emitting GroupMembershipCompleted
positiveExample: GroupMembershipCompleted gates on Branch A only (T101+T102+T103). Branch B (feed seeding) emits FeedSeeded independently. Branch C (notifications) emits independently.
teachingPoint: When parallel branches have different time scales, the completion event gates on the branch that satisfies the core user SLA. Feed seeding and notifications are best-effort background operations. Coupling them in the completion gate multiplies latency for the join confirmation.
discriminatingConstraint: GroupMembershipCompleted emitted when T101 (access)+T102 (record)+T103 (gate) complete. FeedSeeded and MemberWelcomeNotified are NOT in the gate condition. There is no code path where GroupMembershipCompleted waits for Branch B or C.
keywords: time decoupled completion gate branch A only GroupMembershipCompleted feed notification independent

negativeExample: T99 returns VALIDATION_FAILURE for all error cases — the caller cannot distinguish not-found from banned from duplicate
positiveExample: T99 returns distinct error codes: GROUP_NOT_FOUND, USER_BANNED, ALREADY_MEMBER (check ACTIVE+PENDING), INVALID_INVITE_TOKEN. Each code routes to a distinct UI/retry action.
teachingPoint: Validation errors must use distinct codes that match the routing decision. A single VALIDATION_FAILURE code collapses all error paths into one — the caller cannot determine whether to retry, redirect, or show an invite form. Distinct codes enable routing-dependent UI and retry logic.
discriminatingConstraint: T99 returns exactly one of: GROUP_NOT_FOUND, USER_BANNED, ALREADY_MEMBER, INVALID_INVITE_TOKEN. VALIDATION_FAILURE is never returned. Each code has a distinct downstream route.
keywords: distinct error codes routing dependent validation GROUP_NOT_FOUND USER_BANNED ALREADY_MEMBER INVALID_INVITE_TOKEN
